prevalence.value: ~15-30% (children under 5; varies by region and socioeconomic status)
prevalence.source: Brazilian nutrition/child health survey syntheses; WHO anemia estimates context
prevalence.trend: slowly decreasing with nutrition programs; persistent inequality-driven pockets

incidence.value: Common; highest in infancy and early childhood

burden.dalyPerCapita: moderate at population level due to high prevalence; impacts cognition and growth
burden.mortalityRate: Indirect; increases vulnerability to infections and developmental outcomes
burden.source: IHME/GBD anemia context; WHO child nutrition

riskFactors: exclusive cow’s milk intake in infancy
vulnerablePopulations: children with recurrent infections or parasitosis
epidemiology.diagnosticCriteria: Hemoglobin below age thresholds plus iron studies (ferritin with CRP context, transferrin saturation); dietary history; evaluate parasitosis or chronic inflammation when indicated

publicHealthSystem.screeningCoverage: opportunistic hemoglobin screening in primary care and child health visits (variable)
publicHealthSystem.estimatedPatients: Millions of children affected at some point
publicHealthSystem.controlRate: Moderate; improves with supplementation programs and adherence

regionalSpecificities.diagnosisCriteria: Primary care hemoglobin testing; confirm iron deficiency where possible; integrate with growth monitoring
regionalSpecificities.treatmentProtocol: Oral iron supplementation, diet diversification, delayed cow’s milk introduction in infancy, deworming where appropriate; treat underlying causes
regionalSpecificities.regionalChallenges: Adherence (GI side effects), limited lab testing in some areas, overlapping infections and food insecurity
regionalSpecificities.strongPoints: Feasible low-cost treatment and prevention via primary care and nutrition programs
